journal_entry.description: Inventory destroyed by typhoon — $2,000 loss recognized
journal_entry.rationale: A typhoon (natural disaster) has physically destroyed inventory. Under IFRS (IAS 2 — Inventories), when inventory is destroyed, the carrying amount must be written off. The loss is recognized as an expense (debit: Loss on Inventory Write-Off, 1 expense-increase line per debit tuple slot c=1), and the corresponding asset (Inventory) is reduced (credit: Inventory, 1 asset-decrease line per credit tuple slot d=1). No tax lines apply as this is an internal write-off / involuntary loss event — no purchase or sale is occurring, so no HST/GST is triggered.

The word "typhoon" unambiguously identifies the cause as a natural disaster. Enriched to help downstream agents distinguish this from routine inventory shrinkage or disposal.

journal_entry.description: Sale of inventory for cash — revenue recognition and cost of goods sold
journal_entry.rationale:
This is a standard inventory sale requiring two simultaneous entries under IFRS (IAS 2 & IAS 18/IFRS 15):

1. SALES SIDE — Debit tuple (1,0,1,0,0,0) and Credit tuple (0,0,1,1,0,0):
   - Debit a=1 (Asset increase): Cash increases by the $500 selling price received.
   - Debit c=1 (Expense increase): Cost of Goods Sold increases by the $300 carrying cost of the inventory sold.
   - Credit c=1 (Revenue increase): Sales Revenue increases by the $500 selling price.
   - Credit d=1 (Asset decrease): Inventory decreases by the $300 cost of the goods sold.

2. TAX: No tax rules were provided, so no HST/GST lines are added.

journal_entry.description: Convert $100 preferred stock to common stock
journal_entry.rationale: This is an equity reclassification — no cash or assets change hands. The preferred stock balance is reduced (equity decrease → debit) and common stock is increased (equity increase → credit). Per IFRS (IAS 32), conversion of preferred shares to common shares is recorded at the carrying value of the preferred stock being retired. No tax applies to equity-to-equity conversions. Debit tuple (0,0,0,0,1,0) = 1 equity decrease line; Credit tuple (0,1,0,0,0,0) = 1 equity increase line. Total debits $100 = total credits $100.
